conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children you're inquiring about for Child Benefit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I've got two children I'd like to ask about. There's Alex, who's 4 years old, and Blake, who's 14. I'm hoping to find out if I can claim for both of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you assess eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

To start, do you live with Alex and Blake in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes – in Bristol actually. Blake lives at home with me, but Alex lives with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Let me ask about Alex first, since they live with your ex-partner.

Do you make any financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep, such as paying for clothes, food, pocket money, or contributing to their accommodation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex. I help out with things like clothes, some food when I see them, and bits and pieces they need. I try to make sure I'm still doing my part even though they don't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. To determine if your contributions are sufficient for Child Benefit eligibility, I need to understand the amount you're contributing.

Are you contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment (which is approximately £25.60 per week for the eldest or only child, or £16.95 per week for additional children) towards Alex's upkeep? This could be through regular payments or the equivalent value in things like clothes, food, and other items for Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, definitely. I contribute around £50 a week towards Alex, so that's more than the Child Benefit amount. I want to make sure they're properly looked after even though they're not with me day-to-day.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's very helpful, thank you. One more important question about Alex: is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Nobody else is getting it for them at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for that information. Now let me ask about Blake, who lives with you.

Is Blake currently in education or training? And just to confirm, are there any special circumstances I should know about, such as Blake being in hospital, care, or any other living arrangement for extended periods?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake's just at school doing their normal secondary education – they're 14 so still got a couple of years to go yet. No special circumstances really, they just live at home with me. Not in hospital or care or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

1. Are you or anyone else already claiming Child Benefit for Blake?
2. Is Blake working 24 hours or more per week, or receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody's claiming Child Benefit for Blake at the moment. And no, Blake doesn't work – they're just focused on school. They don't receive any benefits either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.**

Here's the summary:

**For Alex (age 4):**
Even though Alex lives with your ex-partner, you are eligible because you're contributing approximately £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep. This exceeds the Child Benefit amount, and according to the rules, you're considered responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care (through food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you can claim it.

**For Blake (age 14):**
You are eligible because Blake lives with you in the UK, is under 16, attends school, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. Blake is not working 24+ hours per week or receiving benefits in their own right.

**Important note:** Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child, so you'll be the claimant for both Alex and Blake.

**One thing to be aware of:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. You can still claim Child Benefit, but you might have to pay back some or all of it through your tax return.

You can now proceed to make your claim for Child Benefit for both children!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement. Although Alex lives with your ex-partner, you are contributing approximately £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child, or £16.95 for additional children). According to the eligibility rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (for example on food, clothes or pocket money). Additionally, no one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement. Blake lives with you in the UK and attends secondary school. Blake is not working 24 hours or more per week and is not receiving any benefits in their own right. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK, and you'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Bristol) and has two children: Alex (4 years old) and Blake (14 years old). Blake lives with the claimant and attends secondary school with no special circumstances. Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, but the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Both children meet the age requirements and the claimant meets the responsibility criteria for both children under the Child Benefit rules. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.
